coredata.dc:title: Doping MoS2 with Graphene Quantum Dots: Structural and Electrical Engineering towards Enhanced Electrochemical Hydrogen Evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jinxue
Zhu, Haifeng
Sun, Yanfang
Tang, Lin
Zhang, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Nano-engineering is an effective strategy to achieve improved catalytic performance. A specific high-performance electrocatalyst of graphene quantum dots doped MoS2 nanosheets with defect-rich structures and improved electrical conductivity, referred as GQDs-MoS2, has been rationally designed and successfully developed through a facile hydrothermal method. XRD, SEM, TEM, and Raman techniques are employed to reveal the formation and the structural features. The doping of GQDs into MoS2 nanosheets plays the key role in enhancing the catalytic activity by creating abundant defect sites both in the edge plane and the basal plane, as well as enhancing the electrical conductivity. Endowed with this, the sample delivers remarkably improved catalytic properties for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction, including the large cathodic current (10mAcm−2 at a small overpotential of 200mV and 74mAcm−2 at a small overpotential of 300mV) and low onset potential (140mV).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen evolution reaction
MoS2
Graphene quantum dots
Electrocatalysis
Nano-engeering

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic synthetic process to prepare defect-rich GQDs-MoS2 and defect-free pristine MoS2 nanosheets.
(a) TEM images of GQDs. The inset shows the corresponding HRTEM image. (b) Digital photos of GQDs solution under (left) visible light and (right) 365nm UV light after several weeks.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum of GQDs-MoS2. (c) Survey XPS spectrum of GQDs-MoS2. High resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, and (f) Mo 3d.
(a) SEM images and (b) the EDS elemental mapping images of GQDs-MoS2.
(a, b) TEM images and (c, d) HRTEM images of GQDs-MoS2. The inset in (b) shows the corresponding interlayer distance. The inset in (d) shows the corresponding FFT pattern.
CV curves of (a) GQDs-MoS2 and (b) MoS2 at PBS ranging from −1.5 to 1.5V.

(a) Polarization curves of various samples and (b) the corresponding Tafel plots of various samples as indicated. (c) EIS spectra of GQDs-MoS2 and MoS2. (d) Polarization curves of the initial cycle and after 1000 cycles obtained from GQDs-MoS2. The inset shows the durability test of GQDs-MoS2 and MoS2 at the η20.
Electrochemical parameters of GQDs-MoS2 and the reported MoS2 catalysts.

head.ce:title: Doping MoS2 with Graphene Quantum Dots: Structural and Electrical Engineering towards Enhanced Electrochemical Hydrogen Evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Science Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Science Analysis of Tumor Marker (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science and Technology, Agricultural University of Hebei, Cangzhou 061100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-engineering is an effective strategy to achieve improved catalytic performance. A specific high-performance electrocatalyst of graphene quantum dots doped MoS2 nanosheets with defect-rich structures and improved electrical conductivity, referred as GQDs-MoS2, has been rationally designed and successfully developed through a facile hydrothermal method. XRD, SEM, TEM, and Raman techniques are employed to reveal the formation and the structural features. The doping of GQDs into MoS2 nanosheets plays the key role in enhancing the catalytic activity by creating abundant defect sites both in the edge plane and the basal plane, as well as enhancing the electrical conductivity. Endowed with this, the sample delivers remarkably improved catalytic properties for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction, including the large cathodic current (10mAcm−2 at a small overpotential of 200mV and 74mAcm−2 at a small overpotential of 300mV) and low onset potential (140mV).

ce:section.ce:para: Catalyst-driven electrochemical water splitting has been considered as one of the cleanest routes for hydrogen production. On the road to explore low-cost and high-performance catalysts for electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), transition metal dichalcogenides have stimulated tremendous research interests due to their specific two-dimensional feature and catalytic ability [1–5]. Among them, MoS2 attracts increasing attention because of its low overpotential and high HER catalytic efficiency [6–8]. The catalytic efficiency of MoS2 is mainly determined by two factors: the electrical conductivity and number of active sites. To improve the electrical conductivity, one generally adopted method is to couple MoS2 with carbon materials, including grapheme [9–11], carbon nanotubes [12], and mesoporous carbon [13]. On the other hand, 1T phase MoS2 catalysts with good electrical conductivity have been prepared for high-performance HER applications [14–16].

More recently, computational and experimental studies have revealed that the edge plane of MoS2 layers is electrochemically active while the basal plane is chemically inert [6,17–19]. Some approaches have been devoted to increase and/or expose the edge sites of MoS2 catalyst, thus obtain more active sites and enhanced catalytic activity [20–22]. However, these edge sites only possess very low ratio compared with the basal plane due to the 2D sheet features, and the total improvement in catalytic activity via this strategy would be limited. Therefore, the fundamental increase in the numbers of active sites should surmount the increase on merely the edge sites, which requires introducing active sites into the basal plane of MoS2. Unfortunately, the synthesis of MoS2 nanosheets with abundant basal active sites is extremely difficult, and relevant researches have rarely been demonstrated and poorly understood [23].

Here, we highlight a rationally designed strategy to accomplish the targets of engineering defects into both the edge plane and the basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets. Moreover, the enhanced electrical conductivity is simultaneously obtained for the composites catalyst. We achieve the exciting improvements by enchasing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into MoS2 nanosheets, thus introducing numerous defects in both the basal and edge planes. And the GQDs with high electrical conductivity are well embedded into MoS2 and will certainly increase the conductivity and intrinsic catalytic activity of the composites catalyst. Based on the unique tailored nanostructures, the present graphene quantum dots doped MoS2 nanosheets (GQDs-MoS2) exhibit excellent HER electrocatalytic performance.

Graphene oxides (GO) are prepared following the method reported in our previous report [24]. The synthesis of GQDs is according to a reported method [25]. 200mg of GO is dispersed in 30mL of DMF under ultrasonic and then transferred into 40mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After heated at 200°C for 8h, the solution is filtered with a 0.22μm microporous membrane to obtain the GQDs dispersion in DMF. DMF is removed using a rotary evaporator at 80°C under reduced pressure. Then the obtained GQDs, 200mg Na2MoO4·2H2O, and 400mg thioacetamide are dispersed in 90mL DI water. The mixture is transferred into 120mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 190°C for 24h. After collected with centrifugation, the resultant GQDs-MoS2 is washed with ethanol and water in turn and vacuum dried at 80°C.

The crystallographic information of GQDs-MoS2 is collected from X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Philips X’-pert X-ray diffractometer). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) are conducted on a JEOL JSM-7500F. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) is performed on a JEOL, JEM-2100. Raman spectroscopy is obtained on a confocal microprobe Raman system (LabRam-e010, 632nm as the excitation source). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is obtained from a RBD upgraded PHI-5000c ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Mg Kα radiation (hν=1253.6eV).

The electrochemical measurements are performed on CHI760D (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) using a conventional three-electrode cell. 4mg GQDs-MoS2, 50 uL Nafion solution (5wt.%), 0.75mL water, and 0.5mL ethanol are mixed under ultrasonic to prepare the GQDs-MoS2 catalyst ink. 5μL of the ink is drop-casted onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with the diameter of 3mm. The HER catalytic property is evaluated by linear sweep voltammetry tests in 0.5M H2SO4 (purged with pure N2). GCE modified with catalyst, graphite rod, and saturated calomel electrodes (SCE) are employed as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. The potentials are calibrated by a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). The catalytic activity of pristine Pt electrode is also conducted using the same method for comparison. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests are obtained on a CHI760D (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 50mM, pH=7.2) at a scan rate of 0.1mVs−1 between −1.5 to 1.5V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is obtained at the frequency ranging from 0.1Hz to 100kHz in 0.5M H2SO4 at the overpotential of 300mV.

To engineer the defect-rich structures of MoS2 nanosheets, GQDs are used as dopants to create abundant defect sites in both the edge plane and the basal plane. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the firstly obtained primary MoS2 nanocrystals tend to form defect-free MoS2 nanosheets via the oriented crystal growth. However, the introduced GQDs will connect with the primary MoS2 nanocrystals, presenting their oriented crystal growth and thus resulting in the defect-rich MoS2 nanosheets with abundant defect sites in both the edge and basal planes.

TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 a. The well dispersed GQDs with size ranging from 6 to 15 nanometers can be observed. The inset in Fig. 1a displays the HRTEM image of GQDs. The lattice spacing of 0.25nm is detected from that, which should be assigned to the characteristic (1120) plane of GQDs [25]. Fig. 1b shows the GQDs aqueous solution under visible and 365nm UV light irradiation, in which different colors of light-brown and blue-green can be observed, respectively. It is due to the size effect of GQDs [25]. After several weeks, no precipitation can be detected in the GQDs solution, indicating its good stability.

To reveal the crystal structure of GQDs-MoS2, XRD pattern is obtained in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the broad peak with low intensity located at 2θ of ∼25° corresponds to the (002) diffraction peak of GQDs, confirming the existence of GQDs in the composites catalyst [26]. In comparison with the standard XRD pattern of 2H-MoS2 (JCPDS Card No. 73-1508), the (002) peak located at 14.4° cannot be detected in our present sample. Instead, two new peaks appear at low 2θ of∼8.6 and 17.5°, which should be due to the first- and second-order reflections from the (002) plane of layered MoS2, respectively [27]. It is consistent with the results recently reported for MoS2 nanosheets with enlarged interlayer [11,27,28]. In this case, the d spacing of 1.02nm is calculated based on the peak at 8.6°, which should be assigned to the doping of GQDs. Besides, other diffraction peaks can be well indexed to (100), (102), and (110) planes of 2H-MoS2. It is noteworthy that the (100) and (110) planes show obvious asymmetric features, indicating the turbostratic stacking nature of the flakes [27,29]. The disappearance of the diffraction peaks at high 2θ region reveals the disordered structure and abundant defect sites on the basal plane of MoS2. Fig. 2b displays the Raman spectrum of GQDs-MoS2, in which the typical Raman peaks of GQDs can be observed at 1365cm−1 and 1598cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. This also confirms the successful doping of GQDs. The relative intensity of “disorder” D band and the crystalline G band (I D/I G) of the GQDs is as low as about 0.7, indicating the high quality of the present GQDs [30]. The peaks of MoS2 are centered at 375cm−1 and 420cm−1, assigning to the E1 2g and A1g vibrational modes, respectively [11]. Interestingly, the Raman peaks located at 295cm−1, 343cm−1, 780cm−1, and 920cm−1 are also detected, which could be assigned to the Mo-O3 and Mo-O vibrations of MoO3 [11,28,31]. The presence of the Raman peaks for Mo-Ox bonds should be attributed to the good coupling between Mo and oxygen function groups of the GQDs, which not only reveal the doping of GQDs into MoS2 rather than simply composited, but also assure the rapid electron transfer between them. XPS measurements are conducted to reveal the composition of the as prepared GQDs-MoS2. As shown in the wide scan XPS spectra of Fig. 2c, four distinctive peaks corresponding to S 2p, Mo 3d, C 1s, and O 1s can be observed at around 162, 230, 285, and 532eV, respectively, indicating the formation of GQDs-MoS2 composite. The Mo to S atomic ratio is about 2, in consistent with the composition of MoS2, and the ratio of C/Mo is about 0.85. To probe the source of C and O elements, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s and O 1s are shown in Fig. 2d and e respectively. A predominant graphitic C 1s peak at 285eV and an O 1s peak at 532eV are observed, and the O/C atomic ratio is determined to be ∼0.3. All these results confirm the existence of GQDs [26,30]. In the high-resolution Mo 3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f), two well defined peaks of 229 and 232eV are observed clearly, which are assigned to Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 respectively, revealing that the present GQDs-MoS2 preserves the semiconducting 2H phase [4,16].

Fig. 3 shows the SEM and the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of GQDs-MoS2. Typical SEM image (Fig. 3a) shows that GQDs-MoS2 are particles with diameters in the range of hundreds nanometers. Sheet-like nanostructures can be observed from the profile of the particles, suggesting that the particles are assembled with 2D nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 3b, EDS mapping analysis of GQDs-MoS2 unambiguously confirms the presence of Mo, S, C, and O elements. The low amount of O element could ascribe to the oxygen-containing function groups of GQDs. It is in accordance with the Raman result. Noting that, the uniform distribution of C element can be observed over the composites, indicating the homogeneous doping of GQDs into the hybrid composites.

The detailed microstructures of GQDs-MoS2 are characterized by TEM technique. At the low magnification TEM of Fig. 4 a, one can see that the GQDs-MoS2 particles are assembled with rippled 2D nanosheets. In the magnified TEM of Fig. 4b, the nanosheets with folded edges can be observed, revealing the good mechanical flexibility. And the average layers of the nanosheets are 6-10 layers. As shown in the inset, the interlayer distance of ∼1.0nm is detected, which is consistent with the XRD results. Careful TEM investigation reveals that, messy and discontinuous lines are observed upon the edge plane of our present GQDs-MoS2 nanosheets. For comparison, Fig. S2 shows that the pristine MoS2 nanosheets possess parallel lines on the edge plane with the interlayer distance of 0.62nm. Fig. 4c displays the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the edge plane of GQDs-MoS2. One can see that, the GQDs (labeled with red circle) successfully dope into the layers of MoS2 nanosheets, which induces the specific dislocations and distortions between the layers. Reasonably, many defects (labeled with white arrows) can be obtained at the edge sites, revealing that the sample possesses the defect-rich edge plane [11,29]. The HRTEM image of basal plane is shown in Fig. 4d. The doped GQDs can be detected in the basal plane of MoS2 nanosheets, which results in many disordered atomic arrangement into the basal plane. As a result, the defect-rich structures are formed upon the basal plane, which will remarkably increase the numbers of active sites and thus inducing improved catalytic activity. The corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern of GQDs-MoS2 is shown in the inset of Fig. 4d. Besides the (1120) diffraction spot (labeled with blue circle) of GQDs, six independent diffraction arcs can be detected. This specific feature fits neither single crystal with six diffraction spots nor the polycrystal with diffraction cycles for MoS2 crystals. According to the previous report, this should be assigned to the quasiperiodic structure associated with abundant defects of MoS2 [23].

Fig. 5 shows the CV curves of GQDs-MoS2 and MoS2 to reveal their electrochemical features. In both Fig. 5a and b, two oxidation peaks can be detected at the anodic scan, corresponding to the oxidation of Mo and water, respectively. In comparison with pristine MoS2, the two oxidation peaks show slightly negative shift for GQDs-MoS2. This should be assigned to the doping of GQDs. Moreover, the oxidation peak at low voltage region of GQDs-MoS2 shows much higher intensity than that of MoS2. It has been reported that this peak is due to the directly exposed Mo atoms [19]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the GQDs-MoS2 possesses much more active sites than MoS2 due to the GQDs doping engineer, thus will exhibit improved electrocatalytic activity.

To highlight the enhancement of doped GQDs on the catalytic activity of MoS2, electrochemical HER measurements of pristine MoS2 and GQDs-MoS2 are carried out and compared. Fig. 6 a displays that, GQDs-MoS2 electrode delivers much smaller onset overpotential of 140mV than that of 200mV for pristine MoS2 electrode, whereas the GQDs electrode exhibits negligible HER catalytic activity. Noting that, the GQDs-MoS2 exhibits very high HER catalytic activity, delivering an amazing low η10 (overpotential at 10mAcm−2) of ∼200mV. This value equals to the recently reported Ni-Mo-S nanosheets on carbon fiber cloth electrode [27]. When the applied potential is at −300mV versus RHE, remarkable high cathodic current density of 74mAcm−2 can be obtained, which is six times that delivered by pristine MoS2. To further understand the improved catalytic behaviors of GQDs-MoS2 in comparison with pristine MoS2, their corresponding Tafel plots are obtained. As shown in Fig. 6b, GQDs-MoS2 delivers a Tafel slope of 43mVdec−1, which is lower than that of 91mVdec−1 for pristine MoS2. The decrease in Tafel slope is due to the faster proton discharge kinetics on GQDs-MoS2 electrode. Although the reaction mechanism and the rate-determining step cannot be exactly determined, the obtained Tafel slope is close to 39mVdec−1 of Heyrovsky reaction and the HER process in our case can be assigned to Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism. Table 1 summaries the electrochemical parameters of our present GQDs-MoS2 and the reported state-of-the-art MoS2 based HER catalysts. One can see that, the previous MoS2 based catalysts exhibited the onset overpotential ranges from 100 to 200mV. The value (140mV) of our present sample is lower than most of the pristine MoS2 catalysts [14,18–20], revealing the doping effect of GQDs. The reported best Tafel slopes mainly located at the region between 40 and 55mVdec−1. The Tafel slope of 43mVdec−1 delivered by GQDs-MoS2 stands among the best results for MoS2-based catalysts, indicating its excellent catalysis property. It is worth noticing that, GQDs-MoS2 delivers higher current density than most of the reported catalysts [9–11,14,15,20–23,27]. More importantly, GQDs-MoS2 manifests a very fast increase in current density. For instance, the current density increases remarkably from 10mAcm−2 to 74mAcm−2 when the potential increases from 200mV to 300mV. This is much better than our recently reported O-MoS2/G and the Ni-Mo-S/Carbon fiber cloth catalysts [11,27]. It is even slightly higher than MoS2 film reported by Lu and co-workers [22], which possesses an ultrahigh current increasing as rapid as 56mAcm−2 per 100mV. This remarkable catalytic feature should be assigned to the abundant active sites in both edge plane and basal plane. All these thorough comparisons substantially highlight the excellent catalytic performance of our sample.

EIS measurements of GQDs-MoS2 and MoS2 are performed to reveal that the doping of GQDs into MoS2 could facilitate the electrical exchange during the catalytic reaction. In Fig. 6c, GQDs-MoS2 exhibits a charge-transfer impedance (RCT) of 91Ω, which is much lower than 537Ω of pristine MoS2, proving the effect of GQDs doping. It is believed that, the RCT can reflect the kinetics of electrocatalysis HER reaction [27,28]. Therefore, the remarkably decreased RCT value of GQDs-MoS2 can result in improved catalytic activity, which is in consistent with the HER results.

Stability is another crucial factor to evaluate the potential application of electrocatalyst. To assess this, a continuous long-term operation of 1000 cycles is conducted for GQDs-MoS2 electrode. As shown in Fig. 6d, the polarization curve obtained after 1000 cycles shows no obvious difference in curve shapes and onset potential in comparison with the initial test. Moreover, the HER durability of GQDs-MoS2 electrode is further evaluated by testing at the η20 (overpotential at current density of 20mAcm−2) for 30000s. Continuous H2 bubbles can be observed on the surface of GQDs-MoS2 electrode throughout the test process (video S3). As shown in the inset of Fig. 6d, faint current density decay can be observed for GQDs-MoS2 after the long durability test, delivering a high current retention of ∼91% compared with its initial current density. It is much better than pristine MoS2, which retains around 74% of its initial current density.

In summary, we have rationally designed and successfully fabricated the graphene quantum dots doped MoS2 nanosheets via a hydrothermal process. The defect-rich features have been explored and achieved in both edge plane and basal plane due to the hybrid engineering of GQDs with MoS2 nanosheets. In addition, the obtained composite catalyst possesses improved electrical conductivity due to the doped GQDs. Therefore, remarkably enhanced electrocatalytic HER properties, including low onset overpotential, high catalytic efficiency, and good stability, have been obtained from the present GQDs-MoS2 compared with pristine MoS2. We believe that the catalytic activity of GQDs-MoS2 can be further boosted by optimizing the composition, morphology, and the constructed building blocks. More importantly, the developed engineering strategy in this work could be applied for the synthesis of other GQDs doped 2D metal oxides/sulfides for high-performance applications in energy conversion and storage, catalysis, and sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (ZR2014EMM004, ZR2014JL015) for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2016.05.148.

ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
